nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of lagoons and seaward reefs, including outer reef slopes. Generally observed at depths of 2–30 m (recorded 0–35 m).

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding primarily on small benthic crustaceans picked from coral crevices with its elongated, beak-like snout, plus brittle stars, molluscs, and small fishes. In captivity, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, chopped meaty seafood (shrimp, squid, marine fish flesh), and quality marine pellet or flake foods. Tends to feed best when offered 2–3 small meals per day given its active swimming and high metabolic demands.

summary: An active, elongated Indo-Pacific wrasse easily recognised by its slender beak-like snout. Generally considered semi-aggressive and not reef safe with small invertebrates, and best housed in large FOWLR systems with a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes: Bird wrasses are protogynous hermaphrodites — individuals begin life as females and may transition to terminal-phase males under social conditions. Sexual dichromatism is pronounced: females and juveniles show a dull brownish anterior with a pale belly, while terminal males develop the characteristic uniform green-to-blue coloration sometimes marketed as the 'Green Bird Wrasse'. Juveniles look noticeably different from adults and may lack the prominent elongated snout, so they are occasionally mistaken for a separate species. Reef compatibility is widely considered borderline — they have been reported to prey on ornamental shrimp, small crabs, feather dusters, tubeworms, and other small motile invertebrates, so they are generally not recommended for invertebrate-stocked reefs. Has been reported as a prolific jumper; a tightly fitting lid is widely considered essential. Tends to be most successful when added to an established tank with plenty of open swimming space and live rock providing hiding spots. When attempting pairs, the female should generally be introduced before the male. Tank size guidance varied across sources: LiveAquaria recommends 130 gallons (~492 L) and Reeflings recommends 300 L; the larger value of 500 L was selected on the basis that Gomphosus varius is an active, fast-swimming species that reaches roughly 30 cm and is reported to need ample swimming space. Temperature ranges differed slightly across sources (LiveAquaria 22–26°C, FishBase 24–28°C, Reeflings 24–27°C); a blended range of 23–27°C was used.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria is generally considered not achieved under typical conditions, although group spawning has been documented in the wild. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, sex change is driven by social hierarchy.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source: tank size (130 gal / ~492 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (easy), temperament (semi-aggressive), reef compatibility (no), diet (carnivore), adult size (~11 in / ~28 cm), prolific-jumper warning, female-before-male pairing.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae), Indo-Pacific range (Cocos-Keeling to Hawaii, Marquesas, Tuamoto; replaced by G. caeruleus in the Indian Ocean), depth (0–35 m, typically 2–30 m), maximum size (30.0 cm SL), temperature (24–28°C), habitat (coral-rich lagoon and seaward reefs), diet (small benthic crustaceans, brittle stars, molluscs, small fishes), and IUCN Least Concern. FishBase notes 'no sex change documented' which conflicts with hobbyist and Wikipedia sources reporting protogyny; the protogynous trait is well-established for the genus and is recorded here.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed protogynous hermaphroditism, sexual dichromatism (female: dull brownish with pale belly, red-orange upper snout; male: uniform green/blue with darker head), juvenile vs adult coloration difference, and maximum size (~30 cm). Cross-checked Labridae family and Indo-Pacific tropical/subtropical range.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (300 L — averaged with LiveAquaria's ~492 L and biased upward to 500 L per active-swimmer guidance), temperature (24–27°C), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), maximum size (~32 cm), semi-aggressive and territorial temperament, protogynous hermaphroditism, and invertebrate predation (used to flag reef compatibility as 'no' rather than 'with caution').

fish.breedingNotes: Generally considered not achieved in home aquaria. In the wild, spawning occurs in pairs or small groups; sex change is socially mediated.
